role_summary: Daughter of NAACP Youth Council leader Clara Luper; participated as a child in the August 19, 1958 Katz Drug Store sit-in in Oklahoma City, an early organized sit-in of the U.S. civil rights movement.
ai_reading: Hildreth's top semantic neighbor is the joint Geraldine Crawford Bennett / Toni Breaux / Willie Elliot Jenkins interview (entry 46, count 74); her remaining top-four neighbors are at much lower counts (27-32), suggesting a shorter or less topic-dense oral history than most catalog entries. Her position on the Individual Conscience vs Collective Discipline axis is +0.66, well toward Collective Discipline, consistent with a child-participant whose testimony narrates a coordinated family-and-NAACP-Youth-Council action rather than an individual conscience arc. The 1958 Katz Drug Store sit-in she participated in is one of the earliest organized sit-ins of the civil rights movement, predating the more-famous February 1960 Greensboro Woolworth's sit-in by sixteen months; the embedding's relatively quiet neighbor signal may reflect the fact that the catalog under-represents the pre-1960 Oklahoma City and Wichita sit-in cohort relative to the Greensboro-and-after sit-in network.
biographical_paragraph: Marilyn Luper Hildreth was a child participant in the August 19, 1958 Katz Drug Store sit-in in downtown Oklahoma City, the action her mother, NAACP Youth Council leader Clara Luper, organized with members of the Council [src: 2]. The sit-in was one of the earliest organized sit-ins of the U.S. civil rights movement, predating the more-famous February 1960 Greensboro Woolworth's sit-in [src: 2]. Independent biographical sourcing on Hildreth's later career is pending; the Library of Congress oral history at entry 82 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) is the primary archival source for her own account [src: 1].
movement_context: The August 19, 1958 Katz Drug Store sit-in that Hildreth joined as a child took place at the corner of Main and Robinson in downtown Oklahoma City, with a group of Oklahoma City NAACP Youth Council members her mother Clara Luper had spent more than two years drilling in nonviolent direct-action methodology [src: 3]. Clara Luper's training curriculum drew on Bayard Rustin's organizational design for the Fellowship of Reconciliation and the 1947 Journey of Reconciliation interracial bus-tour model; the Youth Council ran weekly role-play sessions in which adult members and older youth simulated the abusive responses participants would face at the lunch counter, conditioning the children to maintain composure under verbal assault, food being poured on them, and threats of arrest [src: 3, 6]. The Katz Drug Store at Main and Robinson desegregated its lunch counters within two days of the August 19 action, and Katz subsequently integrated its full chain across Oklahoma, Missouri, Kansas, and Iowa, an outcome that converted the Youth Council action into a regional commercial-policy change rather than a single-location protest [src: 7]. The Oklahoma City Youth Council then turned to the John A. Brown department store, the largest downtown Oklahoma City retailer, whose lunch counter Clara Luper's group picketed across multiple campaign seasons from 1958 through 1961 before the store integrated [src: 3, 6]. The historiography of the sit-in movement records the Oklahoma City Katz action as one of three pre-Greensboro sit-ins that established the tactical template the February 1960 Greensboro Woolworth's four would scale into a national movement: the July 1958 Wichita, Kansas Dockum Drug Store sit-in led by Ron Walters of the Wichita NAACP Youth Council (whose action ran roughly a month before Oklahoma City and which Walters and the Wichita State University Library Special Collections institutional record document as the chronologically first of the modern sit-in wave); the August 1958 Oklahoma City Katz action led by Clara Luper's group; and the November-December 1959 Nashville, Tennessee training-action sit-ins led by James Lawson's Nashville Christian Leadership Conference workshops [src: 4, 6]. The conventional civil-rights timeline that opens with Greensboro on February 1, 1960 obscures the eighteen-month Wichita-Oklahoma-City-Nashville arc that produced the operational methodology Greensboro inherited, and the children Clara Luper trained, Hildreth and her brother Calvin Luper at catalog entry 10 among them, were the human substrate of that arc [src: 3, 6].
legacy_and_reception: Hildreth has remained an active participant in Oklahoma City civil rights commemoration in the decades since the 1958 action, speaking at memorial events, anniversary observances, and historical-society programs about the Katz Drug Store sit-in and her mother's organizing legacy [src: 6, 8]. The Clara Luper Civil Rights Center, established by the Oklahoma City NAACP and Oklahoma Historical Society organizations to document and preserve the records of the Youth Council era, is the principal institutional repository for the family's organizing arc and for the surviving Youth Council participants' testimony [src: 8]. The U.S. Postal Service named the post office building at 305 Northwest 5th Street in Oklahoma City the Clara Luper Post Office Building in 2020 (Public Law 116-159), an institutional recognition that placed her mother's name on permanent federal infrastructure in the city where the 1958 action took place [src: 7]. Hildreth's oral history at the Civil Rights History Project (AFC 2010/039) carries the project's standard audit-tier designation; the Library of Congress American Folklife Center holds the canonical recording and transcript, and the project's downstream re-analysis catalogs the interview within the pre-1960 Oklahoma City sit-in cohort alongside her brother Calvin's interview at entry 10 [src: 1].

gallery.alt: The preserved Greensboro F.W. Woolworth lunch counter at the Smithsonian National Museum of American History
gallery.caption: The preserved lunch counter from the F.W. Woolworth store in Greensboro, North Carolina where the February 1, 1960 sit-in began, now held by the Smithsonian Institution National Museum of American History. The Oklahoma City Katz Drug Store sit-in that Marilyn Luper Hildreth participated in as a child on August 19, 1958 predated the Greensboro action by sixteen months and was one of three pre-Greensboro sit-ins (with the July 1958 Wichita Dockum Drug Store sit-in and the November-December 1959 Nashville training-action sit-ins) that established the tactical template the Greensboro Four would scale into a national movement.

gallery.alt: United States Postal Service 37-cent commemorative stamp issued in 2005 honoring the 1960 Lunch Counter Sit-Ins
gallery.caption: The USPS 37-cent commemorative stamp issued July 1, 2005 in the To Form a More Perfect Union series, depicting a three-dimensional National Civil Rights Museum exhibit by StudioEIS of nonviolent activists at a segregated lunch counter. The stamp dates the lunch counter sit-in tradition to 1960, the Greensboro year; the Oklahoma City Katz Drug Store action that Hildreth and the Clara Luper NAACP Youth Council carried out in August 1958 belongs to the eighteen-month pre-Greensboro arc the federal commemorative tradition routinely elides.

gallery.alt: Life-sized museum diorama depicting four seated African-American demonstrators at a segregated lunch counter, recorded for the National Scenic Byways Program
gallery.caption: Federal Highway Administration / National Scenic Byways Program documentation photograph of a life-sized museum exhibit depicting four African-American demonstrators seated at a segregated lunch counter, held in the National Archives and Records Administration collection. The exhibit format reproduces the Greensboro Four tableau; the Oklahoma City Katz Drug Store sit-in Hildreth joined in August 1958 placed her and the other children Clara Luper trained at a lunch counter in the same posture eighteen months before the Greensboro action the exhibit canonizes.
gallery.photographer: U.S. Department of Transportation, Federal Highway Administration
gallery.repository: National Archives and Records Administration (via Wikimedia Commons)
